title: D1 Digital Tool Navigation and Interface Cause-Effect
short: computer science entry node for digital tool navigation and interface cause-effect
purpose: The learner uses basic digital tools and recognizes that specific interface actions produce specific outcomes rather than treating screens and controls as unpredictable magic.
exit: uses simple digital interface actions intentionally in bounded tasks
predicts what a basic click, tap, drag, or typed input will do in familiar contexts
describes simple input-output cause-effect in a digital environment
succeeds across more than one device or application context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: use taps, clicks, selections, and typed input with clear intent
name what changed on screen after an action
distinguish successful navigation from random trial only
connect a control or icon to its likely function in bounded cases
failure: random-interface-search
learner explores screens by random clicking without stable action-outcome reasoning.
control-function confusion
learner confuses what a button, icon, or input action is for.
input-output disconnect
learner does not connect their action to the resulting change on screen.
device-context fragility
learner succeeds in one interface only and cannot transfer to another bounded context.
interventions: guided action-and-outcome routines that name one interface action and one resulting change explicitly
icon and control matching tasks that connect common interface elements to functions
before-and-after screen comparisons that force explicit input-output talk
paired practice across two simple device or app contexts with the same action logic
items: Why is clicking several places quickly not the same as intentional navigation?
Intentional navigation predicts what an action should do and checks the resulting change instead of clicking randomly until something happens.
How can you tell what a basic interface control is for?
A strong answer uses the icon, label, or observed effect of the control to infer its bounded function.
What should you describe after typing into a simple input box?
You should describe what was entered and what changed in the interface as a result of that input.
noteSecure: Uses basic interfaces intentionally and explains simple action-outcome relations across bounded digital contexts.
noteEmerging: Can sometimes operate a tool but still relies on random search and weak input-output explanations.

title: P1 Sequencing Commands and Programmable Action
short: computer science entry node for sequencing commands and programmable action
purpose: The learner follows and creates simple ordered command sequences for a programmable agent or environment without treating instructions as an unordered list.
exit: follows a bounded sequence of computing commands correctly
creates or revises a simple ordered sequence to achieve a target outcome
explains why order matters in a programmable task
succeeds across more than one unplugged or digital programming context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: execute simple command sequences in order
identify when a target fails because the order is wrong
describe a program as a set of steps rather than a wish or goal
revise one step in a sequence to improve the outcome
failure: order-does-not-matter belief
learner treats commands as if their order can be changed freely without changing the outcome.
goal-step collapse
learner states the intended result but not the executable steps needed to get there.
sequence-tracking instability
learner loses track of where they are in a simple program or command list.
revision-without-cause
learner changes steps but cannot explain what problem the revision addresses.
interventions: sequence enactment routines that compare correct and incorrect command orders
goal-versus-step prompts that force executable action language
step-tracing supports that highlight the current command and next command explicitly
bug-and-revision talk that requires naming the broken step before changing it
items: Why can two correct commands still fail if they are in the wrong order?
Because programmable actions depend on the order of execution, so changing the order can change or break the outcome.
How is a goal different from a program step?
A goal describes what you want to happen, while a program step describes one executable action that helps produce that result.
What should you explain before revising a broken program step?
You should explain what part of the sequence is failing and why that specific step needs to change.
noteSecure: Builds and explains simple command sequences with stable order reasoning and bounded revision.
noteEmerging: Can follow some steps but still treats programs as unordered or goal-only instructions.

title: R1 Symbols, Encoding, and Representation Awareness
short: computer science entry node for symbols, encoding, and representation awareness
purpose: The learner recognizes that symbols, icons, and simple encodings can stand for information or actions without confusing the representation with the thing represented.
exit: interprets simple symbols, icons, and bounded encodings correctly
creates or uses a simple code or representation to communicate information
explains that the same information can be represented in more than one way
succeeds across more than one simple representation context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: interpret icons, symbols, or simple codes with bounded meaning
use one representation to stand for information or action intentionally
compare two simple representations of the same information
avoid confusing the picture, symbol, or code with the underlying content
failure: symbol-object collapse
learner treats a representation as the thing itself rather than as a stand-in.
code-meaning drift
learner loses track of what a simple code or symbol means.
single-representation lock
learner cannot recognize that the same information can appear in another form.
representation-context fragility
learner succeeds with one icon or code set only and cannot transfer to another bounded case.
interventions: match-representation-to-meaning tasks with explicit discussion of stand-in logic
simple encoding games that require stable code-to-meaning tracking
same-information-different-format comparisons across icons, words, and symbols
bounded transfer practice with a second simple symbol or code system
items: Why is an icon not the same thing as the action or object it stands for?
Because the icon is a symbol that represents the action or object rather than being the thing itself.
How can the same message be shown in two different ways?
The same information can be encoded with different symbols, words, pictures, or simple codes while keeping the meaning stable.
What must stay stable in a simple code system?
The meaning of each code element must stay consistent so the message can be interpreted correctly.
noteSecure: Uses symbols and simple encodings intentionally and distinguishes representations from what they stand for.
noteEmerging: Recognizes some icons and codes but still confuses symbols, meanings, and transfer across contexts.

title: I1 Responsible Participation in Digital Spaces
short: computer science entry node for responsible participation in digital spaces
purpose: The learner follows bounded norms for safe, respectful, and responsible technology use without treating digital participation as consequence-free.
exit: follows simple safety and respect norms in digital activities
recognizes that digital actions can affect self and others
makes a bounded responsible choice in a familiar digital-space scenario
succeeds across more than one classroom technology context
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: identify a safe or respectful action in a simple digital scenario
describe one reason a digital action has consequences
distinguish private, shared, and public digital behavior in bounded cases
follow classroom technology routines without avoidable harm or disruption
failure: digital-actions-have-no-consequence belief
learner treats digital actions as if they do not affect other people or the system.
private-shared-public confusion
learner cannot distinguish what should stay private from what may be shared.
respect-norm instability
learner does not apply basic respect routines in digital participation.
rule-repeat-without-reason
learner can repeat a norm but cannot explain why it matters in the bounded case.
interventions: scenario comparisons that make digital consequences visible and discuss who is affected
private-shared-public sorting tasks with simple classroom technology examples
guided digital participation routines that model respectful action and repair
why-this-rule matters prompts tied to one concrete technology-use norm
items: Why can a digital action still matter even if it happens on a screen?
Because digital actions can affect other people, information, devices, or shared spaces even if the action is not physical in the same way.
How is something private different from something shared online?
Private information should stay limited to the right people, while shared information is intentionally available to others in a bounded group or context.
Why is respectful digital participation more than just following a rule?
A strong answer explains how respectful behavior protects people, communication, or the shared digital environment, not just that a rule exists.
noteSecure: Applies simple digital safety and respect norms with awareness that digital actions affect self and others.
noteEmerging: Can repeat some rules but does not yet reason stably about privacy, consequences, or respect in digital spaces.

title: C1 Noticing Errors, Revising Steps, and Persistence in Computing Tasks
short: computer science entry node for noticing errors, revising steps, and persistence in computing tasks
purpose: The learner notices when a sequence or digital action does not work as intended and attempts a bounded revision rather than abandoning the task or randomizing changes.
exit: notices when a computing task does not match the intended outcome
identifies at least one likely source of the error in a bounded case
revises a step intentionally and checks whether the change improved the outcome
persists through more than one simple error-revision cycle
passes a delayed recheck after spacing
evidence: compare intended and actual outcomes in a simple computing task
name one step or action that may have caused the error
revise one part of a sequence instead of changing everything at once
check whether a revision improved the result
failure: all-or-nothing revision
learner changes many steps at once and cannot track what fixed the issue.
error-without-location
learner notices something is wrong but cannot identify a likely source.
abandonment-after-failure
learner stops after an error rather than attempting a bounded revision.
revision-without-check
learner changes a step but does not compare the new result against the target.
interventions: one-change-at-a-time routines that restrict revision to one step per attempt
error-location prompts that compare intended and actual outcomes side by side
short persistence cycles with visible progress markers after each attempt
before-and-after checks that force explicit comparison after a revision
items: Why is changing every step at once a weak way to fix a broken program?
Because it becomes hard to tell which change mattered, so the learner loses evidence about the cause of the problem.
What should you compare when you think a sequence is wrong?
You should compare the intended outcome with the actual outcome and identify which step may have caused the mismatch.
Why should you check the result after revising one step?
Because you need evidence that the change improved the program and to decide what to do next.
noteSecure: Notices computing errors, revises one step intentionally, and checks results with stable persistence.
noteEmerging: Can tell something failed but revisions remain random, untracked, or short-lived.
